title: First-time visitor compares brands from the homepage
goal: Understand the offer from the homepage, open the Cars Brands mega menu, compare one or two brand routes, and keep a clear next step toward reservation.
humanBehaviors: Read the hero before clicking anything.
Open the Cars Brands mega menu instead of typing a URL directly.
Compare at least two brand options before choosing one.
Prefer the most obvious visible CTA over hidden affordances.
successSignals: The mega menu is easy to discover and use.
Brand landings feel distinct enough to support comparison.
One next action is clearly dominant on each step.
The chosen route toward reserve is obvious without rereading copy.
frictionSignals: Too many competing CTAs above the fold.
Brand pages feel interchangeable or confusing.
The user hesitates because the next step is unclear.
Booking intent gets lost during the handoff.

title: SEO visitor lands on a brand page and tries to reserve fast
goal: Arrive cold on an SEO landing, understand the page in one pass, enter dates, and reach reserve without losing context.
humanBehaviors: Skim the headline, price and trust points before scrolling.
Use the first strong booking CTA rather than exploring deep navigation.
Enter dates from the landing itself.
Check whether the reserve page keeps car, dates and times.
successSignals: The landing immediately communicates the model and price guide.
The booking widget is visible and understandable.
Dates and times carry cleanly into the reserve page.
No console or validation noise appears during the handoff.
frictionSignals: The page reads like content only, not a bookable product.
The booking widget feels secondary or hidden.
Date handoff fails or partially resets.
The car or price changes unexpectedly in reserve.

title: Customer compares fleet options before committing
goal: Use the fleet page to narrow the list by dates and brand, compare options, and move into reserve with the selected schedule intact.
humanBehaviors: Set dates before changing filters.
Switch between at least two brands.
Look at visible result counts instead of memorizing cards.
Reserve directly from the filtered card.
successSignals: Filters update the visible inventory clearly.
The selected dates remain visible while comparing brands.
Only the intended vehicle stays visible after filtering.
Reserve receives both the car and the chosen schedule.
frictionSignals: Filters feel sticky, confusing or inconsistent.
The user cannot tell how many cars are actually visible.
Dates disappear during comparison.
The reserve handoff drops the chosen schedule.

title: Customer opens a vehicle detail page and books from there
goal: Use a vehicle page as the last evaluation step, pick dates there, and confirm the reserve page preserves the exact booking intent.
humanBehaviors: Read the lead and quick specs before booking.
Use the vehicle booking form instead of navigating back to fleet.
Expect the same model name and price in reserve.
Treat this as the final decision step, not just content browsing.
successSignals: The vehicle page clearly feels transactional.
The form is easy to complete without extra scrolling confusion.
The reserve page matches the selected model exactly.
Date and time state survives the whole handoff.
frictionSignals: The booking form is visually secondary.
The page looks editorial but not actionable.
Model naming shifts between PDP and reserve.
The reserve page does not inherit the full schedule.

title: Guest completes the reserve flow end to end
goal: Fill guest details, continue through schedule and payment, and complete the success redirect without blocking errors.
humanBehaviors: Fill the form in the presented order.
Expect required fields to explain themselves.
Continue without guessing hidden prerequisites.
Treat a successful payment confirmation as the end of the journey.
successSignals: The step order is obvious and linear.
Required fields unblock at the right time.
Mocked payment completes and confirms the reservation.
The success redirect feels intentional rather than abrupt.
frictionSignals: The user does not know why Continue is disabled.
Field validation appears too late or too vaguely.
Payment step looks broken or half-mounted.
Success feedback feels weak or easy to miss.

title: Customer needs reassurance before reserving
goal: Find a phone, WhatsApp or contact-form path quickly when the user is not ready to reserve yet.
humanBehaviors: Look for visible support channels before filling forms.
Expect one easy contact path in the first viewport.
Use the contact form only after checking the faster channels.
successSignals: Phone and WhatsApp options are visible quickly.
The contact form is easy to find and complete.
Support-oriented pages still suggest a sensible next action.
frictionSignals: Contact options are buried below the fold.
The page asks for too much commitment too early.
The user cannot tell whether WhatsApp, phone or form is preferred.
